$comment: Competitor comparison data for /compare/* pages. Honest — always include when_competitor_is_better. Maintained by programmatic-writer agent. Facts must cite sources; pricing must be current. Update if pricing or positioning changes.

competitors.name: Cara Care
competitors.tagline: Dietitian-backed IBS and gut-health app with a focus on low-FODMAP elimination.
competitors.positioning: Personalized gut-health program led by registered dietitians, strongest for IBS management and FODMAP elimination protocols.
competitors.pricing_notes: Offers free tier with paid dietitian programs — check cara.care/pricing for current rates.

features.name: AI photo analysis of stool
features.note: Cara Care is log-based; PoopCheck classifies directly from a photo.

features.name: Registered dietitian network
features.note: Cara Care's clearest advantage.

competitors.strengths: Cara Care's dietitian network is the real differentiator. If you want a low-FODMAP elimination walked through by a credentialed human, this is the app. Their condition-specific programs (IBS, IBD) are deeper than generic trackers.
competitors.weaknesses_for_user: Cara Care is log-driven — you manually select everything, including Bristol type. That's accurate if you're diligent, but most users aren't, and the data thins out over weeks. There's also no AI visual analysis of stool itself.
competitors.when_competitor_is_better: If you have IBS or IBD and want a guided low-FODMAP protocol with real dietitian support, Cara Care is the better choice. Their depth on condition-specific programs exceeds PoopCheck's.
competitors.when_poopcheck_is_better: If you want effortless daily tracking (photo in, Bristol+color out), a single gut-health score that trends over time, or you're optimizing rather than managing a diagnosed condition, PoopCheck's AI approach fits better.

sources.title: Cara Care website

competitors.name: Dieta Health
competitors.tagline: AI stool-analysis app with published clinical validation from Cedars-Sinai.
competitors.positioning: Clinical-grade AI stool analysis, validated in a Cedars-Sinai study. Focused on research-quality measurement for gastroenterology patients and clinicians.
competitors.pricing_notes: Consumer tier free; clinical tiers for providers. See dieta.health for current pricing.

features.name: AI photo analysis of stool
features.note: Both apps do this. Dieta has published clinical-study backing; PoopCheck has faster consumer-facing UX and broader feature set.

features.name: Published clinical validation
features.note: Dieta's Cedars-Sinai study is a genuine credibility advantage.

competitors.strengths: Dieta Health has something few apps in this category can claim: a peer-reviewed validation study backing its AI accuracy. For clinicians integrating stool data into IBD or post-surgical workflows, that matters. Their clinical focus produces narrower but well-credentialed features.
competitors.weaknesses_for_user: Dieta is more clinical than consumer. If you're not working with a gastroenterologist or managing a diagnosed condition, the app is fewer features and less engaging day-to-day than a consumer app.
competitors.when_competitor_is_better: If you're working with a gastroenterologist who wants clinically-validated tracking data, or you're managing a diagnosed IBD/IBS condition with a care team, Dieta Health's clinical credibility is the right fit.
competitors.when_poopcheck_is_better: If you want consumer-friendly daily tracking, broader features (community, gamification, premium AI chat), or you're optimizing your own gut health without a specific diagnosis, PoopCheck is a better everyday app.

sources.title: Cedars-Sinai: AI app more accurate than patient evaluation of stool samples
Dieta Health website

competitors.name: Balloon
competitors.tagline: Guided gut-health protocols with polished UX, frequently featured in 2026 'best poop tracker' roundups.
competitors.positioning: Protocol-first gut-health app with structured programs (FODMAP, elimination diets, gut-brain exercises). Focused on gentle education and guided action over raw data.
competitors.pricing_notes: Has both free and premium protocol tiers — check balloonguthealth.com for current pricing.

features.name: AI photo analysis of stool
features.note: Balloon is protocol-based; PoopCheck is data-based.

features.name: Guided gut-health protocols
features.note: Balloon's differentiator.

competitors.strengths: Balloon's protocols are its genuine strength — if you want structured guidance (elimination diet, relaxation techniques for gut-brain), Balloon walks you through step by step. UX is polished and approachable.
competitors.weaknesses_for_user: Balloon is prescriptive. If you don't want to follow a program and just want objective tracking, it can feel like hand-holding. Also, it doesn't do AI visual analysis of stool — you log everything manually.
competitors.when_competitor_is_better: If you're new to gut-health tracking and want a guided protocol to follow rather than raw data to interpret, Balloon is the gentler on-ramp.
competitors.when_poopcheck_is_better: If you want automatic Bristol classification from photos, a single daily gut-health score, and the freedom to interpret your own data (with optional AI chat via SOFTie), PoopCheck gets you further faster.

sources.title: Balloon Gut Health website
Balloon: Best iOS poop trackers 2025

competitors.name: Bowelle
competitors.tagline: iOS-only food and symptom diary built specifically for people living with IBS.
competitors.positioning: A food and symptom diary for IBS and digestive health problems. Tracks how you feel, what you eat, stress, bowel movements, water intake, and custom values — manually, privately, on-device.
competitors.pricing_notes: Free to download with a useful basic tier. Premium subscription reported at around $24.99/year on the App Store, sometimes discounted — check the App Store listing for current pricing.

features.name: AI photo analysis of stool
features.note: Bowelle is a manual log; PoopCheck classifies Bristol type, color and consistency from a photo.

features.name: Bristol Stool Scale classification
features.note: Bowelle tracks bowel movements in its diary; the public feature descriptions don't explicitly surface a Bristol scale picker.

features.name: Food + symptom diary
features.note: Food, stress, hydration, mood and custom fields — Bowelle's core strength.

features.name: Custom tracking fields (text / number / tags)
features.note: Bowelle lets you define your own variables and graph them.

features.name: Android availability
features.note: Bowelle is iOS-only; PoopCheck ships on both iOS and Android.

competitors.strengths: Bowelle is a focused, well-loved IBS diary with a long track record on iOS and a loyal user base. The custom-fields system (text, number, tags) is unusually flexible for a symptom tracker, and the on-device privacy model is a genuine differentiator for users who don't want their gut data in the cloud.
competitors.weaknesses_for_user: Bowelle is manual end-to-end — you tap in every food, mood, stress level and bowel movement by hand, which works for diligent users but thins out over weeks. It's iOS-only (no Android), has no AI visual analysis of stool, and no single daily score that summarises your gut day at a glance.
competitors.when_competitor_is_better: If you have an IBS diagnosis, use iOS, and want a flexible, privacy-first manual diary to share with your doctor or dietitian — especially one with custom fields you can tailor to your triggers — Bowelle is the more specialised fit.
competitors.when_poopcheck_is_better: If you want effortless daily tracking (photo in, Bristol type + color + gut-health score out), you're on Android, or you're optimising general gut health rather than managing a diagnosed IBS flare, PoopCheck's AI approach gets you insights with far less logging fatigue.

competitors.name: Plop
competitors.tagline: Power-user poop tracker with deep customization, factor correlation analytics, and on-device privacy.
competitors.positioning: A highly customizable bowel-movement and symptom diary built for people managing IBS, IBD and Crohn's, with factor correlation charts and PDF reports designed to be shared with a clinician.
competitors.pricing_notes: Free to download on iOS and Android with in-app purchases for premium features. Check the App Store and Google Play listings for current subscription pricing.

features.name: AI photo analysis of stool
features.note: Plop is manual logging end-to-end; Bristol type, color and consistency are user-selected. PoopCheck classifies all three from a photo.

features.name: Bristol Stool Scale classification
features.note: Plop lets you set a default Bristol type to speed up logging.

features.name: Customizable tracking factors / categories
features.note: Plop's clearest advantage — define your own factors (pain, mood, sleep, stress, hydration, custom) and chart them.

features.name: Monthly report with PDF export
features.note: Useful for sharing with a gastroenterologist or dietitian.

features.name: On-device photo storage (privacy-first)
features.note: Plop explicitly states photos are not uploaded to a cloud server.

features.name: Android availability
features.note: Both apps ship on iOS and Android.

features.name: Free tier with ads
features.plop: Yes — ads in free tier per user reviews

competitors.strengths: Plop is the deepest-customization tool in this category. The factor correlation matrix and factor-impact-on-plop-count charts give power users who can stomach the manual logging real signal about what triggers their symptoms — the kind of analytics most consumer trackers don't even attempt. The monthly PDF export is genuinely useful for clinic visits, and the on-device photo storage is a real privacy advantage over cloud-synced apps.
competitors.weaknesses_for_user: Plop is manual end-to-end. There's no AI visual analysis of stool, so every Bristol type, color, and factor is tapped in by hand — fine for diligent IBS/IBD users, exhausting for casual trackers. The free tier serves ads after each log, which some reviewers find disruptive, and there's no single daily gut-health score that summarises your day at a glance.
competitors.when_competitor_is_better: If you're managing diagnosed IBS, IBD or Crohn's, want to define your own tracking factors, run correlation analysis on triggers, and bring a polished PDF report to your gastroenterologist, Plop is the better fit. Power users who value depth over speed will get more out of it.
competitors.when_poopcheck_is_better: If you want effortless daily tracking — a photo gives you Bristol type, color, consistency and a single gut-health score in seconds — and you'd rather optimise your gut without becoming a part-time data-entry clerk, PoopCheck's AI approach gets you insights with far less logging fatigue.

plop – poop tracker & analyzer on the App Store
plop – poop tracker & analyzer on Google Play
plop – poop tracker & analyzer on AppBrain (Android stats)

competitors.name: mySymptoms
competitors.tagline: Long-running food and symptom diary with a trigger-correlation engine and a dedicated clinician-share portal.
competitors.positioning: A food, symptom and lifestyle diary by SkyGazer Labs, used to surface diet-related triggers across IBS, IBD, low-FODMAP, migraines, eczema and other chronic symptoms. Their own listing copy: "Helps you explore the connection between your diet and health by easily tracking your meals and symptoms, and sharing detailed journals with your clinicians."
competitors.pricing_notes: Free to download with a 7-day trial. Subscriptions listed on mysymptoms.net at around $9.99/month, $39.99/6 months or $49.99/year (UK: £7.99 / £29.99 / £39.99); correlation analysis is part of the premium tier. Check the App Store, Google Play, or mysymptoms.net/question/prices/ for current numbers.

features.name: AI photo analysis of stool
features.note: mySymptoms is a manual diary across food, symptoms and bowel movements. PoopCheck classifies Bristol type, color and consistency from a photo.

features.name: Bristol Stool Scale classification
features.note: mySymptoms supports Bristol-scale logging as one of its body measures.

features.name: Food + meal diary with database & barcode scanner
features.note: mySymptoms's deepest strength — searchable food database, barcode scanner, and custom items.

features.name: Multi-domain symptom tracking (migraines, skin, sleep, energy, mood)
features.note: mySymptoms is built for tracking symptoms beyond the gut; PoopCheck stays focused on stool and gut health.

features.name: Trigger-correlation engine ("Top Suspects")
features.note: Premium feature in mySymptoms — ranks suspected food→symptom links across the diary.

features.name: PDF / CSV diary report + clinician web portal
features.note: "mySymptoms Clinic" lets you invite a clinician to view your diary directly online.

features.name: Apple Health integration
features.note: mySymptoms's own FAQ states it does not currently integrate with Apple Health.

features.name: Cloud storage with HIPAA & GDPR compliance
features.note: Both apps describe encrypted, regulation-aligned cloud storage.

features.name: iOS + Android availability
features.note: Both apps ship on iOS and Android.

features.name: Free tier without paywalled correlation insights
features.mysymptoms: Trial then paid; correlations behind premium
features.poopcheck: Free, no ads — Premium is optional

features.name: App store rating
features.mysymptoms: ≈4.6★ iOS / ≈4.0★ Android (App Store & Google Play listings)

competitors.strengths: mySymptoms has had a decade-plus to mature its food-and-symptom diary, and it shows: the food database, barcode scanner, and customisable factors make it one of the most complete manual loggers in this category. The "Top Suspects" correlation engine is genuinely useful for surfacing delayed food→symptom links that you'd never spot by eye, and the mySymptoms Clinic web portal gives you a clean, share-with-your-doctor workflow that few consumer trackers offer.
competitors.weaknesses_for_user: mySymptoms is manual end-to-end — every meal, symptom and bowel movement is tapped in by hand, and recent user reviews flag both "tedious symptom entry" and a barcode scanner that often returns generic items rather than the specific brand. The correlation insights that make the app worthwhile sit behind a paid subscription after a 7-day trial, and the app's own FAQ confirms it does not integrate with Apple Health, so daily steps, sleep and other HealthKit data won't flow in automatically.
competitors.when_competitor_is_better: If you're investigating food intolerances, managing IBS/IBD, on a low-FODMAP elimination, or tracking multi-domain symptoms (migraines, skin flares, joint pain) and want a mature correlation engine with a clinician-share portal, mySymptoms is the more specialised tool — and the right pick if you're willing to log diligently in exchange for trigger insights.
competitors.when_poopcheck_is_better: If your main question is about your gut and stool — and you want effortless daily tracking (photo in, Bristol type + color + gut-health score out) without a paid subscription gating the analytics — PoopCheck's AI approach gets you insights with far less logging fatigue, and pulls in Apple Health data mySymptoms doesn't.

competitors.name: Stooly
competitors.tagline: Free, ad-free AI poop and gut tracker built around a single daily photo — no subscription, no hardware, iOS-only.
competitors.positioning: Stooly's own pitch: "AI-powered gut health tracking with just your phone — free, private, and available anywhere you go." The app analyses a single stool photo against the Bristol Stool Scale, returns a gut-health score, and surfaces trends over days, weeks and months. No subscription, no in-app purchases, no ads — the developer (Four Pi Systems LLC) explicitly commits to keeping it free.
competitors.pricing_notes: Stooly is 100% free with no subscription, no in-app purchases, and no ads, per its own website and App Store listing. There is no premium tier to upgrade to.

features.name: AI photo analysis of stool
features.note: Both apps classify Bristol type, color and consistency from a single photo. Stooly is the closest direct feature-parity rival on this core capability.

features.name: Bristol Stool Scale classification
features.note: Both ground their AI in the Bristol scale and surface the type to the user.

features.name: Daily gut-health score
features.note: Both apps roll the per-log analysis into a single gut-health score that trends over time.

features.name: Trend and pattern analysis (days / weeks / months)
AI digestive chat assistant
features.note: Stooly's public materials don't describe an interactive AI assistant — it's analysis + trends, not chat.

features.name: Photo handling
features.stooly: Analysed then discarded — never stored on servers
features.note: Stooly's privacy stance is its clearest differentiator: photos are deleted immediately after analysis.

features.name: Food and lifestyle correlation
features.stooly: Surfaces correlations across diet, sleep and habits in trend view

features.name: Android availability
features.note: Stooly is iOS-only as of May 2026; PoopCheck ships on both iOS and Android.

features.name: Free tier with no ads
features.poopcheck: Free, no ads — Premium is optional

competitors.strengths: Stooly's hard-line stance on price and privacy is the real differentiator. The app is genuinely free with no subscription tier, no in-app purchases, and no ads, and photos are analysed in-flight then discarded — they're never stored on a server. For users who want AI stool analysis but don't want a cloud library of their photos or a subscription prompt, that combination is unusually clean. The product itself stays focused: a photo, a Bristol-grounded score, and a trend view.
competitors.weaknesses_for_user: Stooly is iOS-only, so Android users can't run it at all. Because nothing is stored beyond the derived insight, there's no way to revisit the original photo if you want to second-guess an analysis or share it with a clinician. The feature surface is intentionally narrow — no AI chat assistant, no community, no gamification, and no documented Apple Health integration — so power users who want layered analytics or a coach-style experience will find it thin. It's also a young app (March 2026 listing, very few public reviews), so its long-term roadmap is unproven.
competitors.when_competitor_is_better: If you're on iOS, you want AI stool analysis with the strictest possible privacy posture (photos discarded immediately, no cloud library) and a permanent guarantee of no subscription or ads, Stooly is the cleaner fit. It's also the right pick if you actively want a minimal app and find broader trackers overwhelming.
competitors.when_poopcheck_is_better: If you're on Android, want an AI digestive chat assistant (SOFTie), gamification and community, Apple Health integration, or the ability to keep your photo history for context, PoopCheck is the better fit. PoopCheck's free tier is also ad-free, so you don't have to give up the no-ads experience to get the broader feature set.

competitors.name: Bearable
competitors.tagline: Highly customizable mood and symptom tracker with a correlation engine — it logs bowel movements against the Bristol chart, but manually, alongside dozens of other health metrics.
competitors.positioning: A general-purpose mood, symptom and chronic-illness tracker. Bearable's own pitch: record your "Mood, Emotions, Sleep, Symptoms, & Energy Levels with just a few taps each day," plus endless custom metrics, then surface the correlation between anything you do and how you feel. In 2021 it added a dedicated bowel-movement section — "Add multiple timestamped entries per day based on the Bristol Stool Chart" with a customizable tag or note on each — so gut tracking is one module inside a much broader health tracker, not the whole product.
competitors.pricing_notes: Most features are usable free. Premium is around $6.99/month or $34.99/year (the annual plan is frequently discounted to ~$18.99) with a 7-day free trial, and unlocks correlation reports, custom experiments, unlimited goals, and access to history older than the last 30 days. Bearable also runs a "Bearable Heroes" sponsorship program offering free access to people who can't afford a subscription. Check bearable.app/our-pricing-and-principles for current pricing.

features.name: AI photo analysis of stool
features.note: Bearable logs bowel movements manually against the Bristol chart; PoopCheck classifies Bristol type, color and consistency from a single photo.

features.name: Bristol Stool Scale classification
features.note: Bearable shows Bristol example images to help you pick the type yourself; it doesn't read the photo for you.

features.name: Bowel-movement logging with tags (color, urgency, pain, timestamps)
features.poopcheck: Bristol type + color + consistency from photo
features.note: Bearable supports multiple timestamped entries per day with custom tags and notes.

features.name: Multi-domain tracking (mood, sleep, energy, pain, medication, nutrition)
features.note: Bearable's core strength — it tracks far more than the gut. PoopCheck stays focused on stool and gut health.

features.name: Unlimited custom metrics / factors
features.note: Define and graph your own variables.

features.name: Correlation reports across everything you track
features.note: Bearable's correlation engine connects symptoms to diet, sleep, meds and more.

features.name: Daily gut-health score
features.note: Bearable produces graphs across many metrics but no single gut-specific score.

features.name: Wearable & health-app integrations
features.bearable: Apple Health, Google Fit, Fitbit, Garmin, Oura, Samsung Health
features.note: Bearable's integration breadth is a genuine advantage for whole-body tracking.

features.name: iOS + Android availability
features.note: Both apps ship on iOS and Android.

features.name: Free tier
features.bearable: Extensive free tier; correlations and history beyond 30 days need Premium
features.poopcheck: Free, no ads — Premium is optional

competitors.strengths: Bearable is one of the most flexible health trackers available. You can log mood, sleep, energy, pain, medication, nutrition and any number of custom factors, then let its correlation engine surface what's actually moving your symptoms — and it added a dedicated Bristol-chart bowel-movement section with timestamped entries and tags for color, urgency and pain, so gut symptoms slot neatly into the bigger picture. It's mature and well-loved (4.8★ on iOS across roughly 4,000 ratings, 4.7★ on Android across about 8,600), integrates with Apple Health, Fitbit, Garmin, Oura and more, and runs on both platforms.
competitors.weaknesses_for_user: Bearable is manual end-to-end: you tap in every bowel movement, pick the Bristol type yourself from example images, and add tags by hand — there's no AI that reads a photo for you. Gut tracking is one module among dozens, so it isn't built to go deep on stool the way a dedicated app is — no automatic color or consistency analysis, and no single gut-health score that summarises your day at a glance. The correlation reports and access to history older than 30 days that make the app worthwhile sit behind the Premium subscription.
competitors.when_competitor_is_better: If you're managing a chronic condition across many dimensions — mood, fatigue, pain, sleep, medications and diet, with bowel movements as just one of them — and you want one app with a powerful correlation engine to connect it all, Bearable is the more flexible fit (and its annual plan undercuts PoopCheck Premium).
competitors.when_poopcheck_is_better: If your main question is about your gut specifically and you'd rather not hand-log every detail, PoopCheck's AI does the work — photograph your stool and get Bristol type, color, consistency and a single daily gut-health score in seconds, with no manual Bristol picking.

competitors.name: CleverPoop
competitors.tagline: AI photo-based stool tracker with a wellness-first feel — food, mood and cycle correlations, group leaderboards and a weekly editorial digest.
competitors.positioning: CleverPoop's own pitch: "Gentle AI poop analysis for everyday gut wellness" — a 30-second check-in that uses image recognition to classify each stool against the Bristol Stool Chart, then tracks how it correlates with food, mood, cycle and lifestyle. Built by indie developer Manuel Cadarso, it sits closer to the wellness-app end of the category than to the IBS-clinic end.
competitors.pricing_notes: Free to download on both iOS and Android, with in-app purchases. CleverPoop's release notes confirm both subscription and lifetime purchase tiers are offered; the public listings don't surface a fixed price, so check the App Store or Google Play in-app purchase list for current numbers.

features.name: AI photo analysis of stool
features.note: Both apps classify Bristol type from a photo. CleverPoop frames it as a 30-second check-in; PoopCheck additionally surfaces color and consistency as separate signals.

features.name: Bristol Stool Scale classification
features.note: Both apps ground their AI in the Bristol scale.

features.name: Food + mood + cycle correlation
features.note: CleverPoop's clearest differentiator on tracking: it correlates poop data with food, mood, menstrual cycle and lifestyle factors.

features.name: Advanced symptom metrics (IBS type, bloating, pain level, duration)
features.note: Per CleverPoop's release notes — advanced fields for users tracking IBS-style symptoms in detail.

features.name: Daily gut-health / poop score
features.note: Both apps roll daily analyses into a single number. The vocabulary differs (Poop Score vs. gut-health score) but the function is the same.

features.name: AI digestive chat assistant
features.note: CleverPoop's public materials describe insights and articles, not a back-and-forth AI assistant.

features.name: Public / private groups & leaderboard
features.note: CleverPoop adds private friend-style groups on top of a global leaderboard; PoopCheck offers a global community and leaderboard.

features.name: Editorial gut-health content in-app / on-site
features.clever_poop: The Gut Gazette — weekly articles with clinicians & researchers
features.note: Both publish ongoing gut-health content. CleverPoop bundles theirs into a weekly Gut Gazette; PoopCheck publishes daily on the blog.

features.name: Multi-language support
features.note: CleverPoop's Gut Gazette is published in five languages; PoopCheck's content is English-only today.

features.name: iOS + Android availability
features.note: Both apps ship on iOS and Android.

features.name: Free tier without ads
features.clever_poop: Free download with in-app purchases; public listings don't flag ads
features.poopcheck: Free, no ads — Premium is optional

features.name: Premium pricing transparency
features.clever_poop: Subscription + lifetime tiers exist; prices not published on the public site

features.name: App store rating
features.clever_poop: ~4.4★ on the US App Store (small review base — roughly 11 ratings)
features.poopcheck: 4.8★ combined (~80 reviews across iOS and Android)

competitors.strengths: CleverPoop's most distinctive feature is how broadly it correlates poop with the rest of your day — food, mood, menstrual cycle and lifestyle inputs all feed the same view, so users who suspect hormone- or stress-driven gut changes have a built-in way to see them. The advanced symptom fields (IBS type, bloating, pain level, duration) give it more depth than most wellness-leaning trackers, and the weekly Gut Gazette — published in five languages with clinician and researcher input — is genuine editorial work, not just push notifications.
competitors.weaknesses_for_user: CleverPoop is a young indie app: the US App Store review base is small (about a dozen ratings at the time of writing), the long-term roadmap is unproven, and public pricing isn't transparent — you have to start a checkout flow to see the subscription and lifetime tiers. There's no documented Apple Health integration, so sleep, steps and HealthKit data don't flow in automatically, and there's no AI chat assistant for asking follow-up questions about a result.
competitors.when_competitor_is_better: If you want an AI stool tracker that explicitly tracks menstrual cycle alongside food and mood, an in-app community of public and private groups to compare stats with friends, and editorial gut-health content in your language (Spanish, French, Portuguese or Hindi as well as English), CleverPoop is the better fit. It's also the right pick if a wellness-first, lower-key tone resonates more than a data-dashboard tone.
competitors.when_poopcheck_is_better: If you want transparent published pricing ($6.99/mo or $49.99/yr), Apple Health integration, an AI digestive chat assistant (SOFTie) that contextualises your results, or the reassurance of a larger review base (4.8★ across roughly 80 combined iOS and Android reviews), PoopCheck is the more proven, more transparent everyday app.
